author: @steves7973
text:
A car 's cooling system heats up slowly, so to test a thermostat without damaging it.
Put it in a pad of cold water with a thermometer and heat it up to the set temperature stamped on it.
It should be fully open at the set temperature.
Remember it can also open too early, overcooling the engine. 
Do not boil it or you will damage it.  I've seen it done !

author: @richysee
text:
Place the thermostat itself in a saucepan full of cold water and make sure it is closed. Then heat the water on a stove with a thermometer in the saucepan if possible. As the water comes toward the boil the thermostat should be seen to start opening at about 80 deg Celsius or about 180 deg Fahrenheit and be fully open by the time the water reaches boiling point. It is common to see a cooling system temperature run at slightly above boiling point in the warmer weather. Cooling systems do this easily because automotive cooling systems run under considerable pressure which raises the boiling point of water to as much as 120 degrees Celsius.

Remember, do not open a car or truck radiator cap immediately after an engine has stopped running as above boiling point water will horrifically burn you and disfigure you for life, if you survive.

author: @Kiyarose3999
text: When I used to work on cars we would test the thermostats pouring hot water on them to see if the mechanism worked ok or not. Bit of a no brainer really!.

author: @scotttovey
text:
If after you replace the thermostat you are still not getting heat; hook a garden hose that is attached to the outside spigot up to the exit port of the heater core and hook up an open ended hose to the input port of the heater core.

Turn on the water and slowly open it all the way allowing the full pressure of the water to flush out the heater core. This will loosen up any built up sediment and push it out that a standard engine flush can not deal with.
